text: The present paper focuses on political rhetoric and linguistic spin in speeches delivered by two global leaders in the United Nations General Assembly (UNGA) of 2013: Barack Obama, the President of the United States of America and Hassan Rouhani, the President of the Islamic Republic of Iran. By applying the principle of Systemic Functional Linguistics (SFL) proposed by Halliday, the paper aims at describing and analysing the grammatical resources used by Obama and Rouhani in their speeches with the aim of projecting the ideologies of their respective countries and to exercise soft power. Primarily, the study will discuss the transitivity and modality structures employed by Obama and Rouhani with a view to identifying the national policies projected, the exercise of power and the shaping of international public opinion through their speeches. Through a close examination of the interpersonal and ideational metafunctions of the speeches delivered by Obama and Rouhani at the UNGA, this paper seeks to explain how political agendas of world leaders are packaged into persuasive speeches which, upon interpretation, facilitate the public’s understanding of the underpinnings of international politics.
